fact: July 21 is exactly one month after the summer solstice, which means days are already getting shorter in the Northern Hemisphere - but you probably can't tell yet!
Cicadas are screaming their loudest around this time of year because the heat makes them super active - they can buzz at 120 decibels, louder than a chainsaw!
In many places, this is peak firefly season, and the little glowing bugs are having their biggest dance parties of the year in backyards everywhere.
The sun rises earliest around June 21, but it sets latest around July 1 - so by July 21, both sunrise and sunset times are shifting back toward winter.
Sunflowers in the Northern Hemisphere are reaching their full height right now and their heavy heads are starting to droop because they're packed with seeds.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere countries, July 21 falls smack in the middle of winter, so penguins are huddling together for warmth while we're sweating!
This date falls right in the Cancer zodiac sign period, which lasts until July 22 - Cancer is represented by a crab that carries its home on its back.
Many fruit trees are dropping their early summer fruits around now, creating a sticky, sweet mess on sidewalks that attracts armies of ants and wasps.
July 21 is the 202nd day of the year in regular years and the 203rd in leap years - that means there are exactly 163 days left until New Year's Eve!
Ocean water temperatures in many coastal areas reach their warmest point several weeks after July 21 because water heats up much slower than air.
Mosquitoes are at their most annoying around this time because hot, humid weather makes them breed like crazy in any standing water they can find.
In northern regions like Alaska, the sun barely sets around July 21, creating an eerie twilight that lasts all night long - locals call it the 'white nights.'
